title: Drop & Loot Probability Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Find the real probability of getting rare loot drops. Discover how many kills you need for 90% odds. Plan your farm strategy efficiently.
intro: That item has a **1% drop rate** and you've tried 50 times with no luck. Bad RNG or just normal variance? The answer lies in **binomial distribution**: with 1% drop rate, after 50 attempts you have only **39.5% chance** of getting it. This calculator tells you the real probability of getting at least one drop in N attempts, and how many attempts you need for 50%, 75%, or 90% odds.
keyTakeaway: **Prob(at least 1 drop) = 1 - (1 - dropRate)^attempts.** With 1% drop rate you need **69 attempts** for 50% odds and **230 attempts** for 90%.
useCases: Check if your unlucky streak is normal or if something's broken in the game.
Plan how many hours of farming you need for that rare item.
Compare farming efficiency between different bosses or methods.
Decide whether to keep grinding or switch farming strategies.
Explain to your friend why their bad luck is mathematically normal.

fields.label: Number of Attempts/Kills
fields.help: How many times you've attempted to get the drop.

faq.q: How many attempts do I need for a guaranteed drop?
faq.a: There's no guaranteed drop with pure probability. With 1% drop rate, 69 attempts gives 50% odds, 230 attempts gives 90% odds, and 459 attempts gives 99% odds. Many modern games use 'pity' mechanics for actual guarantees.

faq.q: Why doesn't 100 attempts with 1% drop equal 100% chance?
faq.a: Probability doesn't stack linearly. Each attempt is independent. 100 attempts with 1% drop gives only **63.4%** chance, not 100%. Use the formula: P = 1 - (1 - dropRate)^attempts.

faq.q: What's the formula to calculate drop probability?
faq.a: **P(at least 1 drop) = 1 - (1 - dropRate)^attempts.** To find attempts needed for a target probability P: **attempts = log(1 - P) / log(1 - dropRate).**

faq.q: How do I convert fractional odds to a drop rate?
faq.a: If an item drops '1 in 512', the drop rate is **1 ÷ 512 = 0.195%.** Enter 0.195 in the calculator. For 1 in 100, that's 1%.

faq.q: Is my unlucky streak actually unlucky?
faq.a: If the calculator shows >20% probability of failing your attempts, you're just experiencing normal variance—it happens. Only worry if the odds are below 1-5%.

faq.q: Do games actually use true random drop rates?
faq.a: Most use pseudorandom generators, yes. But many modern games (Genshin Impact, Diablo, etc.) use **pseudo-random distribution** or 'bad luck protection' that increases odds after unlucky streaks.

faq.q: Does this calculator work for gacha and loot boxes?
faq.a: Yes, if you know the actual pull rate. In the US and many countries, games must publish real odds. Use this to see how many pulls you'd need for a target rarity.

faq.q: What's a pity system and how does it affect probability?
faq.a: Pity guarantees the desired item after a certain number of attempts (e.g., guaranteed 5-star in Genshin after 90 pulls). This breaks the pure probability formula—pity guarantees override statistical chance.

explanation:
## Cumulative Drop Probability

That item you're hunting has a **1% drop rate** and you've tried 50 times with no luck. Bad RNG or just normal variance? The answer lies in **complementary probability**: **P(at least 1 drop in n attempts) = 1 - (1-p)^n**.

This is the core formula for all farming questions. It shows that even with decent odds per attempt, you need a surprising number of attempts to guarantee high overall odds.

## Attempts Needed for Each Probability Level

| Drop Rate | 50% Odds | 90% Odds | 99% Odds |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1% | 69 | 230 | 459 |
| 2% | 34 | 114 | 228 |
| 5% | 14 | 45 | 90 |
| 10% | 7 | 22 | 44 |
| 20% | 4 | 11 | 21 |
| 50% | 1 | 4 | 7 |

Notice how low drop rates demand far more attempts. A 0.1% drop requires 693 attempts just for 50% odds!

## The Magic Formula

To find the attempts needed for a target probability P:

**n = log(1 - P) / log(1 - dropRate)**

Example: 50% odds with 1% drop rate:
- log(0.5) / log(0.99) ≈ **69 attempts**

Example: 90% odds with 0.5% drop rate:
- log(0.1) / log(0.995) ≈ **462 attempts**

## When This Formula Works (And When It Doesn't)

**Assumes:** Every attempt is independent with the same probability.

**Breaks when:**
- **Pity timer**: Game guarantees a drop after X failures (common in gacha games)
- **Bad luck protection**: Drop odds increase after each failure
- **Cooldown drops**: Item only drops weekly or monthly (not independent per attempt)
- **Weighted pools**: Different items with different rates (use multinomial instead)

For more on the underlying probability concepts, explore binomial distribution theory.

example.title: Real Example: 1% Drop Rate Over 100 Attempts
example.result: With 1% drop rate over 100 attempts, you have a 63.4% chance of getting at least one drop. If you want 90% confidence, plan for around 230 attempts.
